title: Implement full ProcessAnalogSamples calibration path in repro-cli
description:
Once the byte layout is resolved, replace the simplified 'avg_delta * sample_rate / cal2' current calculation with the full ProcessAnalogSamples algorithm from libenergytracestandalone.so (described in REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md lines 358-366):

  accumulator += (sample - calibLine.offset)
  if accumulator > 0:
      energy_pulses += (accumulator * calibLine.slope)
      accumulator = 0

The calibration lines (slope, offset) come from the response payload of ET_Calibrate. The current et_calibrate() in main.rs only extracts cal1/cal2 as two u32s starting at rx.buf[7]. Re-examine the libjscxds110.so disassembly of ET_Calibrate to determine:

1. The full response payload layout (how many bytes? what fields?).
2. Whether cal1/cal2 are integers or floats.
3. Whether there is more than one calibration line (low-range vs high-range).
4. The exact GetCurrentInNA / pulse-rate-to-µA formula used.

Verify the new calibration against the captures from the data-collection task: the LED-on - busy-loop delta should match the LaunchPad LED drive current within ±10%, and the absolute busy-loop value should be within ±10% of the MSPM0L1306 datasheet RUN current.

Closes the epic when verification passes on both test images.
notes: Update 2026-05-07: simple cal2-based path now produces order-of-magnitude correct readings (~1.1 mA LED-on, ~245 µA no-LED). The previous c4c970c agent claim of ~1.1 µA was a unit conversion bug (divided nA by 1e6 instead of 1e3), not actually wrong calibration math. Remaining work: (a) get ET_Calibrate (cmd=0x1e) responding — tickCount=0 hangs; need valid tickCount values from EnergyTrace_LPRF::_CalibLoads source. (b) implement multi-line (slope, offset) calibration as in TI ProcessAnalogSamples. Both are needed for absolute accuracy beyond order-of-magnitude.

title: Reconcile competing 4-byte sample layouts in REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md using captured raw bytes
description:
REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md contains two contradictory descriptions of the 4-byte sample layout:

A) lines 290-298: byte[0]=0x70 constant, byte[1..2]=16-bit pulse counter, byte[3]=digital flags
B) lines 358-366: 24-bit signed sample = (byte[2]<<16)|(byte[1]<<8)|byte[0], byte[3]=flags

The current repro-cli polling loop uses theory A. The dead-code process_analog_samples function uses theory B.

Using the raw captures from the previous task:

1. Histogram byte[0] across all samples. If theory A is correct it should be exactly 0x70 in 100% of samples (or differ in well-known patterns). If it varies smoothly, theory B is more likely.
2. Plot byte[1..2] as a 16-bit counter (theory A) over time. It should monotonically increase between resets/wraps with deltas proportional to current.
3. Plot the 24-bit signed value (theory B) over time. It should look like a noisy current waveform, with the LED-on capture sitting clearly higher than the busy-loop capture.
4. Determine which interpretation matches the physical expectation that the LED-on test shows ~milliamps more current than the busy-loop test.

Update REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md to remove the wrong interpretation and document the evidence used to disambiguate. This unblocks fixing the calibration math correctly.
close_reason: Byte format resolved: byte[0]=0x70, byte[1]=cumulative 10kHz window counter mod 256, byte[2]=charge pulses since last record (the load signal), byte[3]=flags. Probe self-decimates. Sum byte[2] across all records → total pulses regardless of decimation. 8-byte header is ONLY on URB#1. REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md updated.

title: Capture raw EnergyTrace bytes + computed current from repro-cli on both test FW images
description:
After the two test firmware images from the previous task are built and flashable:

1. Flash et_calib_busy_loop to the LP-MSPM0L1306 via probe-rs (SWD).
2. Run repro-cli for ~10 seconds, capturing:
   - The raw 4-byte samples (hex dump to a file in tools/energytrace/captures/busy_loop.bin)
   - The cal1/cal2 values returned by ET_Calibrate
   - The computed current value the CLI reports
3. Repeat for et_calib_busy_loop_led, output to captures/busy_loop_led.bin.

To do this, first add a --raw-out <path> CLI flag to repro-cli that writes every raw bulk_read payload (after the 8-byte timestamp header) to the file, AND keeps the existing computed-current output on stdout.

Compare:
- busy_loop reading vs MSPM0L1306 datasheet RUN current (~1-2 mA at default clocks).
- busy_loop_led reading vs busy_loop reading. Delta should match LED1 drive current from the LaunchPad schematic (~1-3 mA).

Outcome: a clear data point on whether (a) absolute readings are correct, (b) only deltas are correct (offset wrong but slope right), or (c) both are wrong (calibration totally off).
close_reason: Captures saved at tools/energytrace/captures/{led_on_v2.bin, no_led.bin}. Repro-cli RAW_OUT env var added. Readings: 1.122 mA (LED on), 245 µA (no LED), delta 877 µA — order-of-magnitude correct.

title: Build two test FW images for EnergyTrace calibration: busy-loop and busy-loop+LED
description:
Create two minimal MSPM0L1306 firmware images for known-load EnergyTrace measurements:

1. **busy_loop**: All GPIO configured as input (high-Z pull-down or no pull). cortex-m::asm::nop() in a tight loop. No timers, no peripherals beyond clock init. Expected current: dominated by RUN active current at the post-reset clock (typical 1-2 mA on MSPM0L1306 at default 32 MHz MCLK).

2. **busy_loop_led_on**: Same as busy_loop, but LED1 (PA0 on LP-MSPM0L1306) is configured as Output and driven HIGH so the LED is lit before the busy-loop. Expected current: busy_loop current + LED current. LED1 on the LaunchPad is driven through a series resistor; check schematic to predict drive current (typical ~1-3 mA).

Place under embassy/examples/mspm0l1306/src/bin/ as et_calib_busy_loop.rs and et_calib_busy_loop_led.rs. Use the same project structure as the existing blinky.rs and sleep_demo.rs.

Build verification: both build under nix develop .# --no-pure-eval --command cargo build --release --bin et_calib_busy_loop and ...led with the mspm0l1306rhb feature.

Flashing requires nothing here — flashing is part of the next task. Do NOT spin or use embassy-time; we want a deterministic load with no Timer awaits that could trigger WFE.
close_reason: Two firmware images: et_calib_busy_loop and et_calib_busy_loop_led, build clean under nix develop. Use cortex_m_rt::entry directly to avoid executor WFE.

title: Validate EnergyTrace calibration in repro-cli against known firmware loads
description:
The repro-cli tool (tools/energytrace/repro-cli) reports ~1.1 µA idle, but the calibration math has unverified assumptions. REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md contains TWO competing interpretations of the 4-byte sample (24-bit signed vs 0x70+16-bit-counter), only one of which can be correct. The current path also reduces ET_Calibrate to a single 'cal2' constant, ignoring the multi-line (slope, offset) calibration that ProcessAnalogSamples in libenergytracestandalone.so actually applies.

Goal: validate the calibration end-to-end by flashing test firmware with known load behavior and comparing measured vs expected current. CCS Theia GUI ground truth is NOT available on this host, so validation relies on physics (busy-loop active current vs LED forward current).

Deliverable: repro-cli reports current values within ±10% of expected on at least two distinct loads (idle busy-loop and LED-on busy-loop), and the byte-format ambiguity in REVERSE_ENGINEERING.md is resolved.

title: Decide: skip the custom executor in Phase 1a?
description:
On Cortex-M0+, plain WFE with SCR.SLEEPDEEP=0 (the reset default) already enters MSPM0 SLEEP mode. The generic embassy-executor thread executor (platform-cortex-m, executor-thread) issues WFE after polling and SEV from __pender. SLEEP-mode current savings may therefore be free with no HAL changes.

Question: do we (a) ship the custom executor in Phase 1a as scaffolding for Phase 2 STOP support, or (b) defer all executor work to Phase 2 and make Phase 1a measurement-only?

Tradeoffs:
- (a) Larger Phase 1a, but executor scaffold is ready when STOP support lands.
- (b) Smaller Phase 1a (measurement + sleep_demo only). Phase 2 lands in one self-contained chunk where the executor code is actually justified.

Resolution must be set before Phase 1 implementation tasks can be claimed.
close_reason: Resolved (b): Phase 1a is measurement-only with the generic embassy-executor. The custom executor (low_power.rs + executor.rs + cargo features) moves into Phase 2 where SCR.SLEEPDEEP=1 / PMODECFG actually require it.

title: Phase 1: SLEEP-mode validation with the generic executor
description: Demonstrate that the existing generic embassy-executor (platform-cortex-m, executor-thread — WFE between awaits) already drops the MSPM0 into SLEEP mode on Cortex-M0+ (SCR.SLEEPDEEP=0 is the reset default). Deliverables: a sleep_demo example, hardware idle-current measurement on the LaunchPad, and a clean regression build matrix. No embassy-mspm0 source changes — all executor / low_power scaffolding is deferred to Phase 2 where SCR.SLEEPDEEP=1 / PMODECFG actually require it (decision q4d.1).

title: Resolve ICDI/probe-rs probe-state conflict (currently requires manual replug between flash and measure)
description:
Once probe-rs talks CMSIS-DAP to the XDS110v3, ICDI commands time out until a physical USB unplug/replug. libusb_reset_device() does not work — it removes the probe from the bus permanently until manual replug.

Possible solutions to investigate:
- Send DAP_Disconnect (CMSIS-DAP cmd 0x03) before issuing ICDI commands
- Reset interface 2 alt setting via SET_INTERFACE control transfer
- Find a sequence of ICDI commands that puts the probe back into a clean state from CMSIS-DAP mode
- Investigate using a separate XDS110-only flashing tool (e.g., dslite from CCS) so probe-rs doesn't touch the probe

Until this is fixed, the test cycle is: replug → cargo run (flash) → replug → cargo run repro-cli (measure). One full delta measurement requires two replugs, which is fine for occasional validation but blocks any automated regression testing.

title: Try rebuilding ccs-theia without --enable-components PF_MSPM0 restriction
description: The MSPM0-only install of ccs-theia ships with a placeholder debug adapter (literal 'cmd_start_debugger' command with 'arg_one'/'arg_two' args), preventing any debug session from launching. The real debug-adapter binary appears to live in a component excluded by --enable-components PF_MSPM0. Try removing the component restriction to see if the full install includes a working debug adapter.
close_reason: Removing --enable-components PF_MSPM0 produced a 5.8GB install (vs 2.1GB) but cmd_start_debugger is STILL a placeholder stub returning literal 'arg_one'/'arg_two' args. The bug is in CCS Theia 1.5.1 itself, not in component selection. Two provideDebugAdapterExecutable stubs in app.asar both return cmd_start_debugger; CCSDebugExtensionContribution.resolveDebugConfiguration runs but does not override the executable provider. Reverted nix change to keep the smaller PF_MSPM0-only install.

title: Add EnergyTrace launcher script (Option A: ccs-theia GUI capture)
description: Add tools/energytrace/run.sh that launches ccs-theia with a project-local workspace and captures dir. The script does NOT automate capture (no CLI exists for MSPM0); it just sets up sane paths and prints the in-IDE steps. Discovered while planning q4d.10 measurement after determining no CLI EnergyTrace path exists for MSPM0.
notes: Option A blocked: debug adapter is a placeholder stub. Investigating full install.
close_reason: Option A (ccs-theia GUI capture) is not viable. The script and workspace setup work, but ccs-theia 1.5.1 cannot launch a debug session: provideDebugAdapterExecutable returns a placeholder 'cmd_start_debugger' command, blocking EnergyTrace UI activation (which requires an active debug session). Confirmed unrelated to component selection (mspm0sleep-osu). Keeping tools/energytrace/run.sh + .ccxml in tree as scaffolding for any future Option A retry, but Phase 1 measurement should pivot to Option D (multimeter).

title: Migrate from devenv to standard nix devshell
description:
## Problem

The current Nix setup uses devenv, which constantly causes friction:
- Requires --no-pure-eval for cargo builds
- Doesn't mesh cleanly with standard Nix practices
- Confuses AI agents and humans alike

## Goal

Replace devenv with a standard Nix devshell (e.g., mkShell or devshell) that provides equivalent tooling without the devenv-specific quirks.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] New devshell provides all tools the current devenv shell provides (Rust toolchain, cross-compilation targets, CCS Theia, etc.)
- [ ]  works without --no-pure-eval
- [ ] All cargo builds work inside the new shell
- [ ] Tests verify the new shell works correctly
- [ ] CLAUDE.md updated with new devshell instructions
- [ ] Any other docs updated (README, etc.)
- [ ] Old devenv configuration removed or deprecated

## Notes

Current devenv config is in devenv.nix / devenv.yaml. Check what packages/tools are currently provided and replicate.

title: Package CCS Theia in flake for EnergyTrace sleep validation
description:
Package TI Code Composer Studio Theia 1.5.1 as a Nix derivation in the project flake so the EnergyTrace GUI is reproducibly available for manual sleep-mode validation.

Why: Phase 1 SLEEP-mode validation (mspm0sleep-q4d.10) and later Phase 2 STOP validation need EnergyTrace. Per mspm0sleep-2xm research, the only host-side path on Linux is the CCS Theia GUI; there is no headless API for MSPM0. Packaging it in the flake gives anyone working in this repo a one-command path to the tool.

Scope:
- Fetch CCSTheia1.5.1.00003_linux-x64.zip (~962 MB) via fetchurl from dr-download.ti.com (direct URL confirmed, no click-through).
- Extract zip, run the embedded ccs_theia_setup_*.run installer non-interactively (likely makeself or InstallBuilder; try --mode unattended).
- Wrap in buildFHSEnv with X11/GTK/glib/nss/libusb runtime deps for Electron + bundled JRE.
- Expose ccs-theia binary; add to devenv.shells.default.packages.
- Mark unfree (config.allowUnfree predicate).
- Document udev rules for XDS110 (separate from main package; manual sudo step or NixOS module).
- Iteration expected: FHS dep list + installer flags will need trial-and-error.

Out of scope:
- Headless EnergyTrace automation (impossible with stock TI tooling per mspm0sleep-2xm).
- Packaging older CCS (Eclipse-based) — Theia is current and what TI recommends.
notes:
Working ccs-theia derivation merged in nix/ccs-theia.nix + flake.nix.

Build verified: nix build .#ccs-theia produces a launcher; ccs-theia --version returns 1.5.0 (Theia framework).

Key tricks employed:
1. patchelf the BitRock installer's interpreter to /nix/store linker (same as ti_c2000_nix).
2. buildFHSEnv around the installer: bundled scripts use #!/bin/bash which doesn't exist on NixOS.
3. Race watcher in installPhase: BLACKHAWK sub-installer's bh_driver_install.sh bails (must be root, then fails on /etc/udev/rules.d cp) and propagates a fatal exit. Watcher overwrites it with #!/bin/sh; exit 0 the moment it appears under $out, before bh_emupack invokes it. Other root-required scripts (ti_permissions_install.sh) fail too but parent treats them as non-fatal.
4. Split FHS into installerTargetPkgs (small: bash/coreutils/glibc) and runtimeTargetPkgs (full Electron/X11/libusb): runtime-lib iteration no longer triggers a 5-minute reinstall.
5. --enable-components PF_MSPM0 minimizes payload (BLACKHAWK_EMUPACK still gets dragged in regardless — it's mandatory in the install plan).

Gated as unfree via allowUnfreePredicate scoped to ccs-theia/ccs-theia-unwrapped only; isolated from default devshell via separate pkgsUnfree import. ccs-theia is x86_64-linux only; gracefully omitted on other systems.

Usage: nix run .#ccs-theia (no opt-in penalty for users who don't need it; fetches ~1GB on first build).

Outstanding follow-ups (separate issues):
- udev rules: ship 71-ti-permissions.rules / 99-jlink.rules so XDS110 is accessible without sudo.
- Document EnergyTrace workflow in plan.md (manual SLEEP-mode validation procedure).

title: Research: EnergyTrace on MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad for automated sleep-current regression tests
description:
Investigate whether the MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad (LP-MSPM0L1306) supports TI EnergyTrace technology and whether it can be used to write automated regression tests that verify sleep-mode current consumption.

Scope:
- Does the MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad include EnergyTrace hardware (on-board emulator or external probe support)?
- What is the current measurement range, resolution, and accuracy?
- Is there a documented CLI or scripted interface (e.g., via UniFlash, Code Composer Studio, or MSP430-Flasher) that can capture current measurements programmatically?
- Can the measurement be triggered/automated from a host script (Python, shell) so it could run in CI or a local regression suite?
- What are the hardware/software prerequisites?

Deliverable: Summary of findings with links to relevant docs, plus a recommendation on whether this is viable for automated regression.
notes:
Research complete. Findings:

CONFIRMED — LP-MSPM0L1306 supports EnergyTrace
- Onboard XDS110-ET debug probe includes EnergyTrace technology (TI user guide SLAU869E).
- Power must come solely from the XDS110-ET (board powered via USB, all J101 power jumpers installed) for accurate readings.
- For direct measurement with an external DMM, remove the 3V3 jumper on J101 isolation block and connect ammeter across it.

XDS110-ET (onboard) specs
- Energy profiling range: ~1 µA to 100 mA (base XDS110, no ETHDR).
- Dual-mode current sense, 256 kSPS sample rate.
- Supplies fixed 3.3 V to target.
- Optional ETHDR add-on extends to 700 nA – 800 mA but is a separate purchase, not on the LaunchPad.

EnergyTrace vs EnergyTrace++
- MSPM0 family supports only BASIC EnergyTrace (current/voltage/energy vs time).
- EnergyTrace++ (CPU state + peripheral correlation) is MSP430FR / CC13xx/26xx only — NOT MSPM0.

Tooling
- CCS Theia (GUI) on Linux: Run → Trace → Enable EnergyTrace. Standalone mode works without a debug session. Export → CSV from Summary view.
- No documented headless / CLI interface from TI for EnergyTrace on MSPM0.

Native API question — answered
- 'energytrace-util' uses libmsp430.so (MSP Debug Stack / MSPDS): MSP430_Initialize → MSP430_VCC → MSP430_OpenDevice → MSP430_Run(FREE_RUN) → MSP430_EnableEnergyTrace(cfg, cb) → sleep(N) → MSP430_DisableEnergyTrace → MSP430_Close.
- MSPDS is MSP430/MSP432 ONLY. It cannot open or enumerate MSPM0 (Cortex-M0+) silicon.
- TI publishes NO equivalent native library API for MSPM0 EnergyTrace. MSPM0 uses the closed Java DebugServer + dslite stack; EnergyTrace is exposed only through the CCS Theia GUI plugin (com.ti.energytrace*).
- The XDS110 probe firmware and its USB EnergyTrace endpoint are identical regardless of target family (the analog frontend doesn't care), but the host-side protocol is undocumented for MSPM0.
- Theoretical headless paths (all unattractive): (1) DSS JavaScript against undocumented EnergyTrace plugin classes, (2) reverse-engineer the XDS110 USB EnergyTrace endpoint à la derf/msp430-etv, (3) wait for TI to publish an MSPM0 API.

Implications for our sleep-mode work
- Phase 1 (SLEEP, ~hundreds of µA): well within XDS110-ET range — EnergyTrace is the right tool.
- Phase 2 (STOP, ~30 µA): in-range but near noise floor; expect ±1–2 µA jitter.
- Phase 3 (STANDBY ~400 nA, SHUTDOWN ~150 nA): BELOW 1 µA noise floor. Need external precision DMM/SMU on J101 3V3 header, or the ETHDR add-on.
- No realistic CI automation path with stock TI tooling — treat EnergyTrace as a manual verification step.

Sources:
- SLAU869E LP-MSPM0L1306 User Guide
- SPRUJ01 XDS110 EnergyTrace User Guide
- CCS Theia 1.5 Trace docs (software-dl.ti.com)
- E2E thread 1354988 (J101 mapping)
- carrotIndustries/energytrace-util (libmsp430 usage example)
- derf/msp430-etv (USB protocol RE for MSP430)

title: flake.nix devshell: add thumbv6m-none-eabi rust target
description:
flake.nix devshell currently lists targets = [ "thumbv7m-none-eabi" "thumbv7em-none-eabi" ]. MSPM0 is Cortex-M0+ which needs thumbv6m-none-eabi (the value already set in embassy/examples/mspm0l1306/.cargo/config.toml [build] target). Without it, builds inside 'nix develop' fail to find the right std/no-std crates for the actual chip family.

Action: add 'thumbv6m-none-eabi' to the devshell's languages.rust.targets list. Optional: drop the v7 entries since nothing in this repo targets them.
acceptance_criteria: Inside 'nix develop', 'rustc --print target-list | grep thumbv6m-none-eabi' resolves and 'cargo build' from examples/mspm0l1306 succeeds without errors about a missing target.
close_reason: Added thumbv6m-none-eabi to flake.nix devshell targets; verified rustc resolves target and embassy/examples/mspm0l1306/blinky builds successfully inside nix develop.

title: Phase 2: hardware validation — STOP current + timer accuracy
description:
On MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad:
- Idle current at STOP entry. Plan target ~50uA — verify against datasheet first to set the real expectation (see mspm0sleep-m7s).
- Timer accuracy: 1-second blinks for 60s; compare elapsed wall-clock time to detect drift.
- Wake latency: button press to LED toggle — log microseconds.
- 24-hour stability: continuous STOP/wake cycles, no lockups.

Document measured numbers.
acceptance_criteria: Idle current within 20% of datasheet typ; <0.5% timer drift over 60s; wake latency under 1ms; 24h run completes.
notes: During bring-up: explicitly probe whether SWD/RTT survives STOP. If RTT goes silent during STOP, fall back to GPIO blink patterns to surface state and file a follow-up bead to add enable_debug_during_sleep (gated decision was mspm0sleep-ehe.2, resolved (a) deferred).

title: Phase 2: add Config option enable_stop_mode
description:
Extend embassy-mspm0::Config (lib.rs:152) with:

  pub enable_stop_mode: bool

Default false to preserve Phase 1 behavior. When true, Executor::run() picks stop_sleep() (mspm0sleep-ehe.4) over sleep().

Do NOT add enable_debug_during_sleep — resolved by mspm0sleep-ehe.2 (a), deferred until empirical evidence from bring-up shows it matters.
acceptance_criteria: Config field present, defaults to false, plumbed through to executor's sleep selection; existing init() callers unaffected.

title: Phase 2: extend low_power.rs with STOP-mode entry
description:
Extend embassy/embassy-mspm0/src/low_power.rs (built in mspm0sleep-q4d.5) with STOP-mode entry.

Single configuration in Phase 2 — SYSOSC off, per mspm0sleep-ehe.1 (a)+(c). No StopConfig enum yet.

Names follow SLAU847F vocabulary: function is stop_sleep, register fields named per TRM (no STOP0/STOP1/STOP2 borrowing).

Sequence (per SLAU847F §2.4.2 / Table 2-10):
  - SYSCTL.PMODECFG (0x1140): set DSLEEP = 0b00 (STOP)
  - SYSCTL.SYSOSCCFG (0x1100): set DISABLESTOP = 1 (SYSOSC off in STOP)
  - SCB::set_sleepdeep()
  - dsb / wfi / isb / dsb
  - on wake: SCB::clear_sleepdeep() back to 0

  pub(crate) unsafe fn stop_sleep(cs: CriticalSection)

Mark unsafe — peripherals must be in a safe state for STOP. Wire into Executor::run() guarded by Config::enable_stop_mode (mspm0sleep-ehe.5).

When a future request arrives for STOP-osc-4MHz or STOP-osc-on, introduce a StopConfig enum and select via SYSOSCCFG.{DISABLESTOP, USE4MHZSTOP} — out of scope for this task.
acceptance_criteria: STOP entry sequence is correct per TRM; chip wakes from configured wake source; no register fields written outside their reset-recommended ranges.
notes: No time-driver changes needed in Phase 2 (resolved by mspm0sleep-ehe.3 (a)). LFCLK survives STOP, existing set_alarm race handling at time_driver.rs:241-279 is sufficient.

title: Phase 1: measure SLEEP-mode idle current on LaunchPad with generic executor
description:
On the MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad, with sleep_demo flashed:

1. Capture idle current (ammeter or EnergyTrace) during the 900ms Timer::after window.
2. Compare to a busy-wait equivalent (replace Timer::after with cortex_m::asm::nop loops).
3. Confirm LED blink timing is unaffected (sanity that WFE wake-up isn't dropping timer events).
4. Confirm button press still triggers info! log.
5. Confirm defmt-rtt continues to deliver while CPU is parked in WFE.
6. 1-hour stability run.

Document measured idle current as a baseline for Phase 2 STOP-mode comparison. If the generic executor numbers already meet the 'good enough' bar for some users, that's a real shipped outcome of Phase 1.
acceptance_criteria: All four functional checks pass; idle current measured and recorded; 1h run completes without intervention.

title: Phase 1: regression build matrix
description:
Run a regression matrix covering all L-series dies (per mspm0sleep-9cl resolution: L-series scope), plus a spot-check for one other family to catch the obvious cross-family breakages.

After applying mspm0sleep-8tn (mspm0l1106rhb is not a real feature — use mspm0l1106dgs28 or similar):

  cd embassy

  # L-series (full coverage, build-only)
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1105dgs28
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1106dgs28
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1227pn
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1228pn
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1303rge
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1304rhb
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1305dgs28
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1306rhb       # primary
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1343dgs20
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1344dgs20
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1345dgs28
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1346dgs28
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l2227pn
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l2228pn

  # Other families (smoke check, no functional claim)
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0c1104dgs20
  cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0g3507pm

  # Examples that exist in the tree
  (cd examples/mspm0l1306 && cargo build)
  (cd examples/mspm0l2228 && cargo build)
  (cd examples/mspm0c1104 && cargo build)
  (cd examples/mspm0g3507 && cargo build)

Acceptance: every command exits 0.
acceptance_criteria: All commands above exit 0; no warnings introduced in embassy-mspm0 vs the pre-change baseline.

title: Phase 1: create sleep_demo example using generic embassy-executor
acceptance_criteria: Demo flashes onto the MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad, blinks at the right cadence, and logs on button press.
close_reason: sleep_demo.rs created and builds successfully via nix develop .# --impure --command cargo build

title: Phase 2: wire low_power + executor mod declarations in lib.rs
description:
Edit embassy/embassy-mspm0/src/lib.rs to add:

  #[cfg(any(feature = "executor-thread", feature = "executor-interrupt"))]
  mod low_power;        // private — see q4d.2 resolution (b)

  #[cfg(any(feature = "executor-thread", feature = "executor-interrupt"))]
  pub mod executor;

Note: low_power is NOT 'pub' — only the executor module is public. Plan §Phase 1a step 3 had this wrong on the cfg gate (used 'feature = "executor-thread"' which would have broken executor-interrupt-only builds).

Keep init() and Config unchanged in this task. Backwards compatibility: existing examples must build untouched.
acceptance_criteria: Default-feature build of embassy-mspm0 unchanged; with executor-thread or executor-interrupt the new modules become reachable; existing examples/mspm0l1306 builds still pass.

title: Phase 2: implement embassy-mspm0/src/executor.rs (Executor + InterruptExecutor)
description:
New file: embassy/embassy-mspm0/src/executor.rs, modeled on embassy/embassy-stm32/src/executor.rs.

Required pieces:
- #[unsafe(export_name = "__pender")] gated on any(executor-thread, executor-interrupt)
  - Thread mode: sets a SeqCst AtomicBool (SIGNAL_WORK_THREAD_MODE) — do NOT use SEV
  - Interrupt mode: NVIC pending via cortex_m. armv6m branch must use NVIC::pend (no STIR on M0+)
- pub mod thread (gated on executor-thread): Executor::run() loops poll -> if !signal -> low_power::sleep(cs)
- pub mod interrupt (gated on executor-interrupt): InterruptExecutor; does NOT call sleep()

Re-export 'use embassy_executor_macros::main_cortex_m as main' so users can write #[embassy_mspm0::main(...)] (optional — confirm against stm32 pattern).

NOTE: InterruptExecutor never sleeps; it is polled from interrupt context. Plan wording suggested both modes invoke sleep — they don't.
acceptance_criteria: cargo check passes for both 'executor-thread' and 'executor-interrupt' features in isolation and combined; __pender is generated only once.

title: Phase 2: implement embassy-mspm0/src/low_power.rs (SLEEP via WFI base)
description:
New private module: embassy/embassy-mspm0/src/low_power.rs.

API (private to the crate; not pub since q4d.2 resolved (b) — no manual-sleep() public surface):
  pub(crate) fn sleep(cs: CriticalSection)
    - SCB::clear_sleepdeep()
    - cortex_m::asm::dsb(); cortex_m::asm::wfi(); cortex_m::asm::isb(); cortex_m::asm::dsb()

No 'unsafe' qualifier — bare SLEEP has no peripheral preconditions.

Reference: embassy/embassy-stm32/src/low_power.rs:319-375 (use as a structural model, but drop the unsafe and the StopMode refcounting; STOP-mode entry comes in mspm0sleep-ehe.4).
acceptance_criteria: File compiles under embassy-mspm0 with the executor-thread feature; sleep() is callable from a critical section and produces a WFI instruction in disasm.

title: Phase 2: add embassy-executor optional dep + executor cargo features
description:
Add to embassy/embassy-mspm0/Cargo.toml:

  embassy-executor = { version = "0.10.0", path = "../embassy-executor", optional = true, default-features = false, features = ["arch-cortex-m"] }

[features]
  _executor = ["dep:embassy-executor"]
  executor-thread = ["_executor"]
  executor-interrupt = ["_executor"]

NO separate 'low-power' feature (resolved by mspm0sleep-q4d.2 (b)). low_power module is private and only used by Executor::run.

Verify with cargo check that no existing build breaks.
acceptance_criteria: Default build of embassy-mspm0 unchanged; 'cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1306rhb,executor-thread' compiles to the point where low_power.rs / executor.rs would be expected; embassy-executor only pulled in when an executor feature is enabled.

title: plan.md: §Hardware Test Setup current numbers contradict Phase 1a definition
description:
Phase 1a says SLEEP keeps 'everything else identical to RUN mode' (CPU clock-gated, but the rest — including 32MHz SYSOSC and ULPCLK — keeps running). The §Hardware Test Setup table then lists 'SLEEP (4MHz): ~200uA'. 4MHz is a STOP-with-USE4MHZSTOP detail, not SLEEP. The numbers as written don't match the description.

Action: pull real numbers from the MSPM0L1306 datasheet (https://www.ti.com/document-viewer/mspm0l1306/datasheet, Power section) and replace the table. Phase 1a's '~200uA idle' callout in the body has the same problem and should be re-checked.

title: plan.md: regression matrix references nonexistent feature mspm0l1106rhb
description:
plan.md Phase 1b lists 'cargo check -p embassy-mspm0 --features mspm0l1106rhb'. embassy/embassy-mspm0/Cargo.toml does not define mspm0l1106rhb — only the dgs20/dgs28/rge/rtr/dyy package suffixes for that die. The check command will fail.

Action: replace with a real package suffix, e.g. mspm0l1106dgs28, or pick the suffix that matches the actual board you intend to test on.

title: plan.md: wrong button pin (PA18 vs PA14)
description:
plan.md Phase 1c sleep_demo and §Hardware Test Setup name PA18 as the MSPM0L1306 LaunchPad S2 button. The existing example at embassy/examples/mspm0l1306/src/bin/button.rs:17 already uses PA14 for S2.

Action: confirm against the LaunchPad schematic, then fix the wrong reference (almost certainly the plan).

title: Decide: scope to MSPM0L1306 vs 'full MSPM0 family'
description:
plan.md §Target says 'MSPM0L1306 (first), then generalize to full MSPM0 family.' L-, C-, G-, H-series have meaningfully different SYSCTL bitfields in places (e.g. PMODECFG offsets and SYSOSCCFG bits aren't always identical across families). 'Generalize' implies free work.

Question: does this effort commit to validating per family, or is it L1306-only with downstream issues to track other parts? Resolution shapes Epic Phase 1's regression matrix and the doc citations we collect.
close_reason: Resolved: L-series scope, starting with L1306. Phase 1/2 functional validation runs on the L1306 LaunchPad; build-only validation covers other L-series dies (L1105/1106/1227/1228/1303/1304/1305/1343/1344/1345/1346/2227/2228). L2228 LaunchPad gets a follow-up functional bead since hardware exists. Other families (C-, G-, H-, MSPS003) get follow-up beads if/when hardware is available.

title: Decide: drop time-driver pause/resume work from Phase 2?
description:
plan.md Phase 2 §Time Driver Considerations contradicts itself: it says LFCLK 'stays active in STOP mode (all STOP variants)' and 'no special pause/resume needed for SLEEP', but then proposes adding ensure_alarm_before_sleep() and noting bookkeeping work in time_driver.rs.

The existing set_alarm at time_driver.rs:241-279 already handles the race where the timestamp passes before the alarm arms. If LFCLK truly survives STOP per SLAU847F, the time driver needs zero modifications.

Question: confirm the LFCLK-survives-STOP claim against the TRM, then either (a) drop the time-driver work entirely from Phase 2, or (b) document the actual race we're protecting against.
close_reason: Resolved (a): drop time-driver pause/resume work from Phase 2. LFCLK survives STOP per SLAU847F; existing set_alarm race handling at time_driver.rs:241-279 is sufficient. mspm0sleep-ehe.6 timer-accuracy validation (1Hz blinks for 60s vs wall clock) will catch any drift; if seen, revisit then.

title: Decide: STOP variant naming — TI vs STM32-borrowed
description:
plan.md mixes TI's MSPM0 mode names (SLEEP / STOP / STANDBY / SHUTDOWN, per SLAU847F) with STM32-borrowed labels (STOP0/STOP1/STOP2). MSPM0 doesn't actually have STOPn variants — what plan calls STOP0/1/2 is really one STOP mode with SYSOSCCFG.{DISABLESTOP, USE4MHZSTOP} bits selecting whether SYSOSC keeps running, gears to 4MHz, or shuts off.

Pick a vocabulary and use it consistently across code, comments and docs. Suggestion: use TI naming and reference the SYSOSCCFG bits (e.g. 'STOP-keep / STOP-4MHz / STOP-off') when distinguishing variants.
close_reason: Resolved (a)+(c): use TI TRM names. Phase 2 ships one STOP configuration only — DISABLESTOP=1 (SYSOSC off, lowest current). Variant enum (StopConfig::OscOff/Osc4Mhz/OscOn) deferred until a real use case for the higher-wake-speed configurations appears.

title: Decide: how to host sleep_demo without breaking the shared example crate?
description:
examples/mspm0l1306/Cargo.toml currently enables embassy-executor/{platform-cortex-m, executor-thread, executor-interrupt}. Those features collide with the __pender defined by embassy-mspm0::executor — sleep_demo cannot share a crate with examples that use the generic executor while those features are on.

Options:
(a) Split sleep_demo into its own example sub-crate (examples/mspm0l1306-lp/ or similar) with its own feature set.
(b) Drop platform-cortex-m + executor-thread + executor-interrupt from the shared crate and migrate every existing example onto the mspm0 executor.
(c) Use cargo feature gating in the shared crate (more complex, easy to misconfigure).

Recommendation: (a). Resolve before sleep_demo task is claimed.
close_reason: Obsolete: Phase 1a now uses the generic embassy-executor (q4d.1 resolved (b)), so there is no __pender feature collision with the shared example crate. sleep_demo can live alongside other examples in examples/mspm0l1306.

title: Decide: keep separate 'low-power' cargo feature, or fold into executor-thread/interrupt?
description:
embassy-stm32 has both a 'low-power' feature (exposes sleep() as a public API) and the 'executor-thread'/'executor-interrupt' features that depend on it. This split helps users who call sleep() manually without the custom executor.

Question: do we replicate that split, or just gate everything on executor-thread/executor-interrupt and skip the extra feature?

Pick the split if there's a credible MSPM0 use case for manually calling sleep() outside the executor loop. Otherwise the simpler shape is a single set of executor features.
close_reason: Resolved (b): no separate 'low-power' cargo feature. low_power module is private; sleep() is only invoked from Executor::run. Re-introduce a public feature later only if real demand for manual sleep() shows up — non-breaking to add, breaking to remove.

title: Phase 2: STOP mode + time-driver integration
description: Extend the executor scaffold from Phase 1 with MSPM0 STOP mode (PMODECFG.DSLEEP=00, SCR.SLEEPDEEP=1, SYSOSCCFG configuration). Add the per-call enable_stop_mode config knob and validate that the LFCLK-based time driver continues counting across STOP. Targets ~50uA idle on MSPM0L1306.

title: OPTIONAL: Capture USB traffic with usbmon to anchor EnergyTrace byte format from a TI tool
description:
If reconciling the sample byte layout from raw captures alone is ambiguous, fall back to capturing USB traffic from a TI binary that DOES work with this probe family.

CCS Theia is known not to work on this host, so this task is gated on finding an alternative TI binary that will speak EnergyTrace to a 0451:bef3 XDS110v3.

Candidates to try:
- TI UniFlash CLI (dslite.sh) — may expose EnergyTrace via DSS scripts.
- A standalone build of libjscxds110.so + a small driver program (since we have the source path for the lib in nix store, we could LD_PRELOAD it from a trivial wrapper).
- The 'energytrace-util' tool from carrotIndustries — it targets libmsp430.so for older XDS110, will not work for MSPM0 silicon, but if we can get it to talk to the probe at all (probe init succeeds even if target init fails) we may catch ET_Calibrate response bytes on the wire.

Capture with: sudo modprobe usbmon; sudo cat /sys/kernel/debug/usb/usbmon/<bus>u > capture.txt while running the TI tool.

Lower priority — only pursue if the previous tasks cannot resolve the byte format from raw data alone.

title: Wire XDS110 udev rules for ccs-theia
description:
The ccs-theia install ships 71-ti-permissions.rules and 99-jlink.rules under $out/ccs/install_scripts/. These need to be installed system-wide for the XDS110 debug probe (and J-Link, when used) to be accessible to a non-root user.

Why: Without these rules, the user has to run ccs-theia (and probe-rs) with sudo to access the XDS110 USB device — defeats the point of a reproducible dev shell.

How to apply: Add a NixOS module in nix/ that exposes the rules as services.udev.packages = [ ccs-theia.udevRules ] (need to create a thin udevRules output on the derivation). For non-NixOS users, document the manual sudo install_drivers.sh step.

title: plan.md: delete Phase 3 section, replace with forward-reference to mspm0sleep-qme
description:
Per resolution of mspm0sleep-qme.1 (a): remove the Phase 3 section from plan.md. If any Phase 2 design discussion needs to cite future refcounting work, add a one-line forward reference to the bead 'mspm0sleep-qme' instead of inlining a placeholder section.

Specific change: delete the entire '## Phase 3: Peripheral-Aware Deep Sleep (Future)' block (currently plan.md lines 220-235) and the trailing '---' separator.
acceptance_criteria: plan.md no longer contains a Phase 3 section; any forward references use bead IDs, not inline placeholders.

title: Phase 2 follow-up: hardware validation on MSPM0L2228 LaunchPad
description:
Mirror mspm0sleep-ehe.6 on the L2228 LaunchPad. Same checks (idle current, timer accuracy, wake latency, 24h stability) but on the larger L-series die. Confirms the SYSCTL register layout assumed by mspm0sleep-ehe.4 (PMODECFG @ 0x1140, SYSOSCCFG @ 0x1100, etc.) holds across L-series.

Skip if L2228 hardware unavailable — file as deferred.
acceptance_criteria: Idle current measured and recorded for L2228; timer drift <0.5% over 60s; 24h run completes.

title: Decide: drop Phase 3 from this plan and open as follow-up after Phase 2 lands?
description:
plan.md Phase 3 §No Plan Yet admits the section is a placeholder. Carrying it in the active plan blurs scope.

Question: delete Phase 3 from plan.md and rely on this epic (mspm0sleep-qme) as the follow-up tracker, or keep the placeholder for context?
close_reason: Resolved (a): delete Phase 3 from plan.md. Future direction lives in the Phase 3 epic (mspm0sleep-qme); plan.md describes what's being shipped now. Forward references from Phase 2 sections (if needed) should cite mspm0sleep-qme inline.

title: plan.md: re-verify TRM/app-note page citations against the actual PDFs
description:
plan.md §Documentation References uses tilde-prefixed pages ('p.~265', 'p.~267', 'p.~421-422') suggesting estimates. The PDFs are now downloaded at /tmp/slau847f.pdf (SLAU847F TRM, 22.7 MB) and /tmp/slaae72.pdf (SLAAE72 power app note, 0.5 MB).

Action: open each cited section and replace the tildes with verified page numbers so future agents don't chase stale citations.

title: Decide: defer enable_debug_during_sleep until a STANDBY/SHUTDOWN phase actually needs it
description:
plan.md proposes adding 'enable_debug_during_sleep: bool' to Config in Phase 2 but admits it is 'for STANDBY later'. STM32 wires it to the DBGMCU.CR bits — it's not a no-op there because STOP/STANDBY can drop the debug clock.

Question: does MSPM0 STOP actually drop the SWD/debug clock such that we need this knob in Phase 2, or is it only relevant for STANDBY (Phase 3+)?

If the latter, drop the knob from Phase 2 and add it where it earns its keep.
close_reason: Resolved (a): defer the knob entirely. No enable_debug_during_sleep in Phase 2. Hardware bring-up (mspm0sleep-ehe.6) will tell us empirically whether RTT survives MSPM0 STOP on the LaunchPad — if it doesn't, file a follow-up bead and pick between stub vs real implementation then.

title: Phase 3: Peripheral-aware deep sleep (refcounted StopMode)
description: Future work: track which peripherals require PD1 (e.g. active DMA) and gate STOP entry on a refcount, analogous to embassy-stm32 StopMode. Needs MSPM0 peripheral-by-peripheral analysis (which wake from STOP, what state survives). Deliberately under-scoped; convert to concrete tasks once Phase 2 is in.
